title: Dereferencing
description: Access data through its memory address.

slides.title: De-referencing 🕵️
slides.content:
Since pointers store memory addresses, what if we want to retrieve the actual *value* stored at that address?

To view the data housed within the pointer's memory location, we use the `*` operator again, known as **dereferencing**.

slides.title: Dereference Example
slides.content: Look closely at how * acts as both creator and extractor.

quizData.question: If you wrote `cout << ptr;` without the `*` what would happen?

options.text: It prints a memory address like 0xfa39

quizData.explanation: ptr stores an address. By adding the * (dereference) in front of ptr, you peek inside the address and grab the underlying data ("Pizza").

slides.title: The Dual Nature of * 🌓
slides.content:
Note that the `*` sign has two distinct uses based on context.

1. In declaration (`int* ptr`), it creates a pointer variable.
2. When executed (`cout << *ptr`), it acts as a dereferencing operator to scoop data out of the pointer.

quizData.question: Dereferencing a pointer means looking up the value stored at the memory address the pointer holds.
quizData.explanation: Yes, 'dereferencing' translates an address lookup back into human readable data.
